Nineteenth Century

E. E. Constance Jones

1848 – 1922

A Cambridge logician on attention, dualism, and the logic of character.

I’ll refer to E. E. Constance Jones as “she”: E. E. Constance Jones was Emily Elizabeth Constance Jones, 1848–1922. She did not publish a single systematic treatise titled “philosophy of mind,” so the philosophy of mind has to be reconstructed from several bodies of work: her paper “An Aspect of Attention,” her essay on James Ward’s refutation of dualism, her logic and philosophy of language, her moral psychology of character, and her Sidgwickian work on practical reason.

The best reconstruction is this: Jones’s philosophy of mind is an active, attentional, anti-reductionist, non-Cartesian spiritualistic monism, with a strong panpsychist or panpsychism-adjacent tendency. She rejects both materialism and substance dualism. She treats mind and matter as distinguishable aspects or factors of a single experiential reality, not as two self-sufficient realms. Human consciousness is not the whole mind; it is a narrow, higher-level region of a much larger psychical organism, unified and regulated by attention. Attention includes volition, and it is the central active principle of mind. Feeling, especially pleasure and pain, is the complementary passive or reactive principle. The subconscious is not a marginal curiosity but the background condition of memory, habit, bodily regulation, aesthetic feeling, prejudice, sleep, hypnosis, pain, and multiple consciousness.

1. Intellectual and historical location

Jones belongs to the late nineteenth- and early twentieth-century Cambridge setting in which logic, psychology, metaphysics, and ethics had not yet been separated in the way later analytic philosophy would separate them. She studied at Girton under Henry Sidgwick, James Ward, and John Neville Keynes, translated part of Hermann Lotze’s Mikrocosmos, became Sidgwick’s literary executor, and worked mainly in logic and ethics while also publishing on attention, character, dualism, and moral psychology. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy describes her as a contemporary of Russell and Moore and as a major figure in philosophical logic whose intension/extension work anticipated parts of Frege’s sense/reference distinction and Russell’s pre-“On Denoting” meaning/denotation distinction.

That background matters because her philosophy of mind is not “analytic philosophy of mind” in the post-1950s sense. It is closer to the older field of “mental philosophy,” but she is also already participating in the anti-psychologistic turn that helped form analytic philosophy. Mind, founded in 1876, was at first an organ for both philosophy and psychology, and early contributors included now canonical figures and forgotten transitional figures such as Jones.

Her main intellectual lineages are threefold. From Sidgwick she takes a concern for rigorous distinctions and anti-psychologism in logic. From Ward she takes the critique of naturalism, epiphenomenalism, and mind/matter dualism, together with the emphasis on conation, activity, and the subject-object structure of experience. From Lotze and Leibniz she takes the thought that the psychical is not an accidental late product of dead matter but is somehow continuous through reality. Jones herself explicitly invokes Leibniz and Lotze when arguing that we cannot draw a sharp line at which psychical life ceases and mere matter begins.

2. The deepest metaphysical commitment: anti-Cartesian unity of subject and object

Jones’s most explicit metaphysical statement about mind appears in “Dr. Ward’s Refutation of Dualism” (1900). There she strongly endorses Ward’s attack on Cartesian dualism, psychophysical parallelism, conscious automatism, epiphenomenalism, and mechanical naturalism. The target is not merely the claim that mind and body interact in the wrong way; it is the deeper assumption that reality is first split into two self-contained series, one physical and one psychical, and then we must somehow explain how they correspond. Jones regards this original split as the source of the philosophical problem.

Her alternative begins from concrete experience. In experience, we do not first find “matter” on one side and “mind” on the other. We find a subject in relation to an object. This gives us a duality, but not a dualism. Subject and object are distinguishable, but each is intelligible only within the living unity of experience. Jones, following Ward, says that when we analyze experience we find not a dualism of matter and mind but a duality-in-unity of subject and object.

This is not subjective idealism in the crude sense that objects are private ideas “inside the head.” On the contrary, Jones’s criticism of dualism includes a criticism of the very picture that places experience inside the skin and leaves an external world outside it. The object is genuinely other than the subject, but it is not metaphysically foreign to experience. Subject and object are correlates within one concrete whole.

The upshot is that Jones’s philosophy of mind is neither Cartesian dualism nor reductive materialism. It is a form of spiritualistic monism: once materialism is rejected and dualism is untenable, the most stable view is a unity of experience in which mind, activity, objectivity, and nature are internally connected. Jones’s Ward essay even entertains the idea that finite spiritual subjects and the material universe belong within an all-pervading spiritual order, though she cautiously says she is not sure she has fully grasped all the consequences of the view.

3. Psychophysical relation: concomitance without epiphenomenalism

Jones’s position on the mind-body relation is subtle. In “An Aspect of Attention,” she says that every psychical change has a material correlate and every material change has a psychical correlate. That sounds like psychophysical parallelism, but she does not accept the standard parallelist picture in which two independent series run alongside one another without interaction. Her point is rather that the psychical and the material are inseparable aspects or factors of organized reality.

She is especially hostile to the idea that mind is an ineffective accompaniment of physical processes. In the Ward essay, she argues that if mind is reduced to an ineffective shadow of mechanism, volition and activity disappear from the world. But individual experience contains activity, purpose, and the realization of ends. Human volitions, she says following Ward, affect objects of experience at least as directive forces.

So the correct reconstruction is this: Jones rejects both interactionist substance dualism and causally inert epiphenomenalism. She does not think a nonphysical soul pushes matter from outside the physical order. But neither does she think physical causation is complete in a way that makes consciousness idle. Psychical activity and physical modification belong to a single concrete process. In bodily disease, the “physical” side may take the initiative; in other cases, the psychical side may do so. That is not a violation of her monism; it is her way of saying that initiative can be described from different aspects within one psychophysical system.

4. Panpsychism or panpsychism-adjacent idealism

Jones’s most striking metaphysical claim is that psychical life cannot be confined to human beings, animals, or even organisms in a simple way. She begins “An Aspect of Attention” by emphasizing the connectedness of the material world. If every material system is connected with every other in complex ways, she asks what the psychical counterpart of this connectedness must be. Her answer is that we cannot plausibly draw a line at which psychical life ceases and “mere matter” begins. She says we are pushed toward a Leibnizian or Lotzean conception in which psyche is everywhere, and all material elements have a psychical side or factor.

This should not be read as saying that stones have human-like consciousness. Jones’s view is more graded and systematic. There are systems that are primarily material in our mode of access—gravitational, chemical, circulatory—and systems that are primarily psychical or ideational. But both sides are always present in some form. At the lower end, we know the material aspect more readily and infer the psychical correspondent. At the higher end, in human ideation, we know the psychical aspect more readily and infer the material correspondent.

In contemporary terms, Jones is not a physicalist, not an emergentist in the sense of brute emergence from wholly non-mental matter, and not a Cartesian dualist. She is closest to a dual-aspect panpsychist idealism: every material system has a psychical side, but human consciousness is a highly organized, highly complex, self-conscious, attentional form of that more general psychical reality. The reason she takes this line is clear: if attention and feeling appeared only at some late point in nature, they would seem to emerge inexplicably from wholly non-psychical antecedents. Jones explicitly treats that as an unacceptable consequence.

5. The architecture of mind: attention, feeling, and system

Jones’s psychology is built around two primitive psychical dimensions: attention and feeling. Following Ward, she treats attention, including volition, as the active side of mind, and feeling, especially pleasure and pain, as the passive or reactive side. If there is psychical continuity throughout the world, she argues, that continuity must be of the nature of attention and feeling.

Attention is not merely a spotlight that illuminates already formed contents. It is an organizing, selecting, sustaining, and directing activity. In the psychical region, attention keeps order among many systems, just as material processes coexist in complex but non-chaotic relations. The unity of attention is what holds the psychical side of systems together.

Feeling is not a detachable sensation added to cognition. It is tied to the organism’s hold on its relations. Pain, for example, is not merely a sensory datum; it is a disturbance that forcibly captures attention and prevents its free movement. Fatigue, illness, and bodily disturbance make demands on the available “stock” of attention. This gives Jones a deeply embodied theory of feeling: affective life is bound up with the distribution and disturbance of attention across conscious and subconscious systems.

6. Consciousness is not the whole mind

One of Jones’s most important contributions is her theory of the subconscious. She does not use “subconscious” as a narrow Freudian term. It refers to a vast range of psychical systems that are not presently in clear consciousness but still structure perception, memory, bodily regulation, habit, skill, mood, and judgment.

She argues that subconscious attention differs from conscious attention by degree, not by kind. The psychical activity that maintains lower bodily systems is not normally under conscious control, but it is still attention in a broader sense. This is why digestion, fatigue, bodily illness, and health can affect conscious attention, and why intense conscious work can disturb bodily functions.

Human beings differ from lower systems not because they alone have psyche, but because they have vastly greater complexity, clearer organization, self-consciousness, and a reserve of “free” attention that can be directed now here, now there. Subconscious systems, by contrast, usually have no surplus attention beyond what is needed to maintain themselves.

This gives Jones a layered model of mind. At the bottom are primary psychical-material systems. Above these are organized bodily systems. Above those are ideational systems. Human consciousness is only the most flexible and explicit region of a much larger psychical organization. Skills such as walking, writing, speaking, and playing an instrument begin as conscious acquisitions but become automatic and pass into subconscious functioning, thereby freeing attention for other tasks.

7. Memory, habit, prejudice, and implicit cognition

Jones’s account of subconscious systems has striking implications for cognition. She observes that a person can “feel” that something is mistaken before being able to state the mistake clearly, or feel that something is delightful or objectionable before having an explicit account of why. She explains this by saying that material too complex or unclear for conscious intelligence can nevertheless appeal to subconscious connexions.

The same mechanism explains the force of early association, custom, prejudice, and authority. A person may intellectually accept a new doctrine but still feel that it has not “come home,” because the subconscious system has not yet come into harmony with the explicit judgment. Jones thereby anticipates later distinctions between explicit belief and implicit cognitive-affective orientation, though her own framework is idealist and attentional rather than computational.

Memory is also system-dependent. Environmental cues can reactivate submerged connexions, which is why returning to a place, repeating an action, or hearing a piece of music can restore forgotten contents. Jones treats such cases not as mysterious intrusions but as evidence that cognition depends on networks of subconscious association and attention.

8. Sleep, dreams, hypnosis, pain, and multiple consciousness

Jones’s psychology becomes especially concrete in her treatment of abnormal and liminal mental states. Sleep, on her account, is not a simple absence of consciousness. It is a redistribution of attention. In healthy sleep, attention is released from external objects and redirected toward the subconscious psychical correspondents of bodily renewal, nutrition, and reintegration. Dreamless sleep is especially restorative because attention is not caught near the ideational region but is available for deeper subconscious repair.

Hypnosis differs from ordinary sleep because attention is detached from the normal environment but remains attached to the hypnotizer. This allows Jones to explain suggestion, narrowed awareness, altered sensibility, and trance behavior in terms of redirected or distorted attention rather than in terms of a wholly separate mental substance.

Pain is likewise attentional. Severe pain forcibly holds attention and prevents its free movement. Conversely, intense interest, fear, battle, surprise, or distraction can temporarily block pain by drawing attention elsewhere. This does not mean pain is unreal; it means pain is a psychophysical state whose felt character depends on the enforced direction of attention.

Cases of double personality or multiple consciousness are explained as dislocations of attention across conscious and subconscious regions, always accompanied by physical modifications. Jones normalizes these phenomena by comparing them with ordinary divided activity: reading aloud without grasping the meaning, copying carefully without attending to content, walking while talking, or performing skilled acts while the mind is elsewhere.

9. The self: not an isolated ego, but an organized psychical system

Jones’s moral psychology helps clarify her concept of self. In “Character and Circumstance,” she defines character as the relatively stable psychical complex of habits, feelings, dispositions, and tendencies manifested in conduct. Circumstance includes the material, economic, social, political, moral, religious, aesthetic, and intellectual environment, but she immediately notes that the line between character and circumstance is difficult to draw, which indicates some underlying unity.

The self is not sealed off from its environment. Circumstances are “my” circumstances only insofar as they affect my consciousness; circumstances that do not touch me in thought or feeling are not part of my life. Character and circumstance reciprocally influence one another. Character can resist circumstance, but character can also be formed, altered, damaged, or strengthened through circumstance.

This yields a relational psychology of personhood. Character is psychical and is “me” or part of me, distinguished from the rest of the world with which I interact. Yet the self develops only in relation to the world, and character cannot be improved except through some alteration of circumstance—education, exhortation, environment, social condition, discipline, opportunity, or relation.

Jones is therefore not an atomistic individualist about mind. The person is an organized psychical being whose agency is real but whose development is environmentally mediated. In modern vocabulary, one might say that she combines a robust theory of agency with an equally robust theory of situatedness.

10. Logic, intentionality, and anti-psychologism

Jones’s logic is essential to her philosophy of mind because it prevents her idealism from becoming psychologism. She insists that logic is not the science of mental processes. It is the science of the import and relations of propositions. If we study the mental processes involved in inference, that is psychology, not logic. Logic is objective because it concerns objects of thought, not private mental episodes.

This anti-psychologism is crucial. Jones does not identify the object of thought with an image, idea, or subjective mental equivalent. In her logic, terms refer to objects, and propositions are about objects. Mental equivalents differ from person to person and from context to context; they may be richer for experts and thinner for novices. But these private variations do not determine logical import.

Her famous “law of significant assertion” says, in effect, that informative predication involves identity of denotation with diversity of intension. When we say that S is P, we are not merely repeating S as S; we identify one object or set through different modes of determination. This is why Jones is historically important for the development of philosophy of language: she anticipates aspects of Frege and Russell while working in a nineteenth-century logical idiom.

For philosophy of mind, the point is that Jones has a sophisticated theory of intentionality. Thought is directed toward objects, not toward private ideas as its final object. Language makes public, shareable object-reference possible despite variation in private mental associations. This gives her a hybrid position: metaphysically idealist, psychologically rich, but logically anti-psychologistic.

11. Imagination, fiction, and impossible objects

Jones’s account of intentionality also extends to nonexistent or impossible objects. Recent scholarship on Jones emphasizes her view that we can think and speak meaningfully about such things as a round square. On that view, the round square has a kind of existence in what she calls a “Region of Supposition,” though it does not exist in space. This allows reasoning about impossibilia without saying that spatial reality contains contradictions.

This matters for philosophy of mind because Jones does not reduce imagination to private imagery. To imagine, suppose, or reason about a fictional or impossible object is to stand in an intentional relation to an object of thought with a determinate logical role. The mind is not just a stream of sensations; it is capable of directedness toward objects that may be actual, possible, fictional, or impossible-in-space.

12. Soul and incorporeal mind

Jones does use “soul” language, but the evidence does not support reading her as a defender of a separable Cartesian soul-substance. In “An Aspect of Attention,” the language of soul appears in connection with aesthetic depth: music can affect subconscious connexions deeper than ordinary consciousness. The “soul” there is best read as the whole depth of the psychical organism, not as a detachable immaterial entity.

Her metaphysics does recognize finite spiritual subjects, and in the Ward essay she considers a spiritualistic monism in which finite minds and the material universe belong to an all-pervading spirit. But this is not a doctrine of incorporeal minds floating free of the material order. It is a doctrine that the material order itself is not alien to spirit. Matter is the object-side of a spiritual reality, not an independent dead stuff to which soul is externally added.

The strongest reason not to attribute a standard doctrine of incorporeal souls to Jones is her psychophysical concomitance principle: she holds that psychical and material changes are inseparable. A free-floating finite mind with psychical changes but no material correlate would sit badly with that principle. Her view leaves room for an all-pervading spiritual reality, but not, on the sources considered here, for a standard Cartesian or scholastic doctrine of the individual soul as an independently subsisting immaterial substance.

13. Practical reason and moral psychology

Jones’s ethics is not merely external to her philosophy of mind. Her response to Sidgwick’s “dualism of practical reason” reveals a psychological and rational unity she wants to preserve. In “Practical Dualism,” her aim is to derive or connect prudence, rational self-interest, with benevolence. She argues that the reasonableness of benevolence implies the reasonableness of self-love, thereby trying to reduce the apparent conflict between self-regarding and other-regarding rational principles.

The psychological background is that human beings are not purely isolated centers of preference. Jones holds that we are naturally empathetic and often identify our happiness with the happiness of others, though later commentators have questioned whether that psychological fact yields the full normative conclusion she wants.

For her philosophy of mind, the important point is that conation, feeling, selfhood, and sociality are intertwined. The mind is not a purely cognitive spectator. It is active, interested, purposive, and affectively linked to other subjects.

14. A reconstructed Jonesian philosophy of mind: core theses

The following theses capture the system that can be extracted from Jones’s writings.

First, experience is the fundamental starting point. We do not begin with matter and then add mind, nor with private ideas and then infer an external world. Concrete experience contains a subject-object correlation.

Second, subject and object form a duality, not a dualism. They are distinguishable but inseparable. The object is genuinely other than the subject, but not metaphysically alien to experience.

Third, mind and matter are not independent substances. They are coordinated aspects or factors of one concrete reality. The standard mind-body problem arises from falsely abstracting them into independent series.

Fourth, the psychical is not a late accident of matter. Every material system has a psychical side or correspondent. Human consciousness is a high-level organization of psychical reality, not an inexplicable eruption from the non-psychical.

Fifth, attention is the active principle of mind. It includes selection, direction, organization, and volition. It is the condition of psychical order.

Sixth, feeling is the passive-reactive principle of mind. Pleasure and pain are not mere ornaments of cognition but expressions of how psychical systems are sustained, disturbed, harmonized, or dislocated.

Seventh, consciousness is narrow; mind is wide. Most mental functioning is subconscious. The subconscious includes bodily regulation, automatized skill, memory, association, prejudice, aesthetic resonance, and latent knowledge.

Eighth, human mentality is marked by free attention. Humans possess a reserve of attention that can be redirected flexibly, making self-consciousness, reflection, deliberation, and complex learning possible.

Ninth, mental causation is real but not Cartesian. Volition is efficacious as a directive psychical activity within the unified psychophysical process, not as a ghostly force imposed from outside nature.

Tenth, the self is a psychical organization formed in relation to circumstance. Character is part of the self, but it is developed, expressed, and altered through the environment.

Eleventh, thought is object-directed. Logic is not psychology, and the objects of thought are not reducible to private images or ideas.

Twelfth, language and logic secure public intentionality. Even though mental equivalents vary privately, propositions can have objective import because terms can refer to common objects through differing intensions.

Thirteenth, imagination and supposition are genuine intentional activities. The mind can meaningfully think about nonexistent or impossible objects without thereby making them spatially real.

Fourteenth, soul-language must be interpreted within spiritualistic monism, not substance dualism. Jones’s “soul” is the deep psychical organism or the finite spiritual subject within a larger spiritual order, not a detachable immaterial thing.

15. Historical significance for philosophy of mind

Jones’s place in the history of philosophy of mind is unusual because she stands at a crossroads. She inherits British idealism, Ward’s personal idealist psychology, Lotzean metaphysics, and Sidgwickian ethics, while also helping to shape the anti-psychologistic analytic tradition. Contemporary scholarship continues to recover her work, noting that she published widely in logic, ethics, and moral psychology and anticipated later philosophy of language and logic.

Relative to Descartes, Jones is anti-dualist. Relative to Hobbesian or late Victorian mechanistic materialism, she is anti-reductionist. Relative to Humean associationism, she gives a more active and conative account of mind. Relative to Kant and the idealists, she accepts the activity of the subject but resists reducing objectivity to private subjectivity. Relative to Frege and Russell, she shares the anti-psychologistic concern for logical objectivity but retains a much richer psychology of attention, subconsciousness, affect, and embodiment.

Her theory also has surprising contemporary resonances. Her panpsychist continuity thesis resembles current attempts to avoid brute emergence of consciousness. Her treatment of subconscious attention anticipates later interest in implicit cognition, automaticity, divided attention, and nonconscious processing. Her account of character and circumstance anticipates situated and environmental approaches to moral psychology. Her anti-epiphenomenalism anticipates later worries about mental causation. But she should not be assimilated too quickly to any modern school: her framework is neither computational functionalism, nor identity theory, nor phenomenology, nor orthodox analytic philosophy of mind. It is a late-Victorian idealist-attentional system with a distinctive logic of intentionality.

16. Final assessment

A comprehensive philosophy of mind extracted from Jones would be best named:

Attentional spiritualistic monism with panpsychist continuity and anti-psychologistic intentional realism.

That label is dense, but each part is needed. It is attentional because attention is the central active principle of mind. It is spiritualistic monism because mind and matter are not two substances but belong to one experiential-spiritual reality. It has panpsychist continuity because Jones denies a sharp boundary between psychical life and matter. It is anti-psychologistic because she refuses to reduce logic, truth, and meaning to mental processes. It is intentional realist because thought is directed toward objects, including objects of supposition, rather than merely toward private ideas.

Jones’s importance lies precisely in the combination. She gives us neither a disembodied soul doctrine nor a reductive science of mind. She gives us a layered, active, embodied, subconscious, object-directed, ethically situated mind within a metaphysics that denies both dead matter and isolated spirit.
